coredata.dc:description: Accurate detection of organophosphate pesticides (OPs) is paramount for ensuring food safety. Dendritic mesoporous silica sphere was employed to confine gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@dmSiO2) to ameliorate fluorescent property of AuNCs. A AuNCs@dmSiO2-based fluorescent method was developed for OPs sensing. Identification of Cu2+ by AuNCs quenched AuNCs@dmSiO2 fluorescence. Interaction between Cu2+ and generated thiocholine in catalysis of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) caused fluorescence enhancement. OPs, an inhibitor of AChE, suppressed thiocholine production to cause fluorescence quenching. Based on fluorescent variation, a fluorescent method was proposed for OPs by selecting paraoxon as a model within range of 0.05–25.0 ng/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.032 ng/mL. Besides, a portable test swab was prepared for on-site monitoring OP paraoxon with a smartphone-based 3D-printing portable device with a LOD of 0.65 ng/mL. This work is highlighted by the inspiration of designing highly fluorescent AuNCs, and the provision of a viable avenue for OPs-related food analysis.

head.ce:title: Mediated self-assembled gold nanoclusters with mesoporous silica particles to boost fluorescence for enhanced on-site monitoring of organophosphate pesticides

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate detection of organophosphate pesticides (OPs) is paramount for ensuring food safety. Dendritic mesoporous silica sphere was employed to confine gold nanoclusters (AuNCs@dmSiO2) to ameliorate fluorescent property of AuNCs. A AuNCs@dmSiO2-based fluorescent method was developed for OPs sensing. Identification of Cu2+ by AuNCs quenched AuNCs@dmSiO2 fluorescence. Interaction between Cu2+ and generated thiocholine in catalysis of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) caused fluorescence enhancement. OPs, an inhibitor of AChE, suppressed thiocholine production to cause fluorescence quenching. Based on fluorescent variation, a fluorescent method was proposed for OPs by selecting paraoxon as a model within range of 0.05–25.0 ng/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.032 ng/mL. Besides, a portable test swab was prepared for on-site monitoring OP paraoxon with a smartphone-based 3D-printing portable device with a LOD of 0.65 ng/mL. This work is highlighted by the inspiration of designing highly fluorescent AuNCs, and the provision of a viable avenue for OPs-related food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety remains a perpetual subject of interest for everyone globally. Organophosphate pesticides (OPs) are extensively employed in agriculture to control pests and enhance crop yields. With increasing public concern regarding food safety, the issue of OPs residues has garnered widespread attention (Qu et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2024). OPs residues, entering the body via ingestion, skin contact, and inhalation, can impact the nervous, endocrine, and immune systems, manifesting symptoms such as headaches, nausea, vomiting, and potentially lethal poisoning (Wu et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021). Moreover, long-term exposure to OPs may increase the risk of cancer, reproductive system abnormalities, and neurological disorders (Badr, 2020; Jokanović et al., 2023). Thus, accurate detection of OPs is essential for safeguarding food safety and public health. Traditional techniques, like chromatography (Kang et al., 2023) and mass spectrometry (Zhu et al., 2009) have been utilized for OPs residue detection. Nevertheless, these methods not only demand costly equipment and intricate operational procedures but also mandate skilled operators and may require additional time, potentially limiting their on-site applicability in agricultural settings (Umapathi et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2024).

In recent years, fluorescent sensors have emerged as a novel detection technology, exhibiting unique advantages in detecting OPs (Gyanjyoti et al., 2023; Li, Huang, et al., 2023). Leveraging principles of molecular recognition and fluorescence signal transduction, fluorescent sensors enable accurate and rapid detection of OPs residues (Wang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2023). They offer high sensitivity, timely response, simple operability, and real-time monitoring, making them promising for detecting OPs with broad application prospects and significant practical implications. On the other hand, the development of high-performance fluorescent probes is essential for the advancement of efficient sensors. Certainly, a diverse range of fluorescent nano-probes has been devised, such as CdTe quantum dots (S. Huang et al., 2021), organic fluorophores (Chen et al., 2021), and metal-organic framework (MOFs) (Yuan et al., 2022). Nevertheless, these probes typically exhibit toxicity, insolubility, and photosensitivity.

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are attracting considerable attention due to their intriguing physicochemical characteristics, including robust photoluminescence, non-toxicity, high water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility (H. X. Li, Su, et al., 2024; Sonia et al., 2021; Wang, Han, et al., 2024). Hence, AuNCs are widely employed in biomedical, environmental science, and food industries (Guo et al., 2021; Niazi et al., 2024). Despite their appealing features, the low quantum yield of AuNCs is a major obstacle restricting their widespread application (Guo et al., 2023; He et al., 2023; Li, Zhang, et al., 2024). Fortunately, more recently, the strategy of confinement using host materials, which limits the free movement and enhances the electron-hole pair recombination efficiency of guest fluorescent molecules within the constraints of the host materials, has garnered significant interest in imparting strong fluorescence properties to AuNCs (J. Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2024). Some designed host materials have been utilized to achieve the confinement effect for constructing highly fluorescent AuNCs, such as MOFs (Mu et al., 2023; Wei et al., 2023), hydroxy double salt (HDS) (M. K. Wang, Han, et al., 2024) and cationic polymer nanogel (CPN) (Bai et al., 2018). Despite significant progress, the restricted acid-base stability of these materials has encouraged researchers to seek alternative host materials for AuNCs.

Dendritic mesoporous silica sphere (dmSiO2) has attracted considerable attention in sensing applications mainly due to its advantages such as high pore volume, excellent biocompatibility, favorable surface properties, and high controllability (L. Huang et al., 2018; Xiao et al., 2023), which inspires us to use it as the extremely promising host materials for AuNCs. Here, the immobilization of AuNCs into dmSiO2 (AuNCs@dmSiO2) was designed by a straightforward electrostatic assembly strategy for the first time. As expected, the confinement effect of dmSiO2 ameliorated the fluorescent property of AuNCs, including high quantum yield (QY), long fluorescence lifetime, and good stability. The as-prepared AuNCs@dmSiO2 could accumulate Cu2+ adsorption benefiting from the porous property of dmSiO2, and the identification of Cu2+ by AuNCs caused a significant fluorescence quenching. Besides, the thiocholine (TCh), a hydrolysis product generated by acetylcholinesterase (AChE), can coordinate with Cu2+, bringing about a fluorescence recovery. Interestingly, the specificity inhibition toward AChE by OPs can suppress the production of TCh to result in the fluorescence quenching of the AuNCs@dmSiO2. Therefore, a AuNCs@dmSiO2 based sensitive fluorescent analysis platform for OPs detection by selecting paraoxon as a representative of OPs was creatively proposed (Scheme 1 ). In addition, a portable test swab loaded with AuNCs@dmSiO2 was established, demonstrating significant benefits in visual, equipment-free, and quantitative detection of OPs when integrated with a smartphone platform. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study on projecting highly fluorescent AuNCs@dmSiO2-based composites for detecting OPs with robust performance.

Acetylcholinesterase (AChE, from electrophorus electricus), glucosidase (GLU), and cholesterol were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), tyrosine (Tyr), triethanolamine (TEA), acetylcholine chloride (ATCh), glutathione (GSH), tetraethyl orthosilicate, sodium salicylate, valine (Val), (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (TEOS), and serine (Ser) were gotten from Aladdin Inc.. Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), pepsin, galactose, glucose, polyphenol oxidase (PPO), sucrose, creatinine, and urease were bought from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. Proline (Pro), ammonia, aspartic acid (Asp), methionine (Met) and other reagents (of analytical purity) were sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) analysis was accomplished on a Frontier PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometric measurements were performed using a PerkinElmer UV/VIS Lambda 365 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence measurements were carried out with a PerkinElmer FL 6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) were collected by the JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on a JEOL JSM-7610FPlus microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) valence band spectra was conducted on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were conducted on a Rigaku-Smart Lab 3KW. Zeta potential measurements were conducted by a Litesizer 500 analyzer. Fluorescence decays were performed with a Fluorescence Lifetime and Steady State Spectroscopy (FLS920, Edinburgh Instrument). All pictures were taken by an iPhone 14. All pH measurements were conducted using a lei-ci PHS-3C pH meter. All temperature measurements were accomplished by using a water bath.

The preparation steps of AuNCs and dmSiO2 were available in the Supplementary Material (L. Huang et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2012). For the preparation of AuNCs@dmSiO2 composite, briefly, 2 mL AuNCs (1.0 mg/mL) was mixed with 1 mL dmSiO2 (10.0 mg/mL) and stirred for 5 min. Subsequently, the solution was by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min. And the collected precipitation was freeze dried for the further use.

For the analysis of OPs (paraoxon), 40 μL paraoxon with different concentration, 12 μL AChE (1.0 U/mL), 20 μL ATCh (1.0 mM) and 28 μL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 500 μL, 20 mM) were mixed and allowed to incubate at 37 °C for 40 min. Then, 60 μL Cu2+ (0.1 mM), 340 μL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 6.0, 500 μL, 20 mM) and 500 μL AuNCs@dmSiO2 (1.2 mg/mL) were introduced into the afore mentioned mixtures. After full incubation for 2 min, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using a single excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of portable swab kit for OPs
ce:section.ce:para: For the construction of portable test swabs, a mixed solution consisting of AuNCs@dmSiO2 (1.2 mg/mL in the 20 mM Tris-HCl buffer with pH of 6.0) and gelatin (5.0 mg/mL) was added onto the medical swabs. After permeating for 10 min, the fabricated test swab was freeze-dried for the subsequent pesticide detection. For OPs analysis by the swabs, the afore mentioned procedures in Section 2. 4 were conducted, and a test swab was soaked into the reacting solution of OPs/AChE/ATCh/Cu2+. Optical images were captured using a smartphone (iPhone 14) and subsequently analyzed through a color identification app (Color-Collection) to quantitatively identify OPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of OPs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Real samples including apple juice, tap water, and Chinese cabbage from local supermarket were utilized as research subjects to validate practical applications of this method and the swab kit. Briefly, the tap water sample (10 mL) was filtered by a 0.22 mm membrane. Apple juice and Chinese cabbage was extracted from commercial apple and cabbage. The analysis of OPs in the real samples was evaluated using the standard addition method and performed according to the procedures outlined in the buffer system and swab kit. Besides, a UV–Vis method was adopted as a reference method to determine the OPs in real samples.

All fluorescence measurements were conducted in triplicate, and the final result was derived by calculating the average values along with their respective standard deviations. Data were processed and analyzed by Origin 9.0 (Origin Lab Co., MA, USA). All measurements for FL, UV–Vis, and zeta potential were conducted in triplicate, and a representative measurement from each sample is presented herein, following a thorough assessment of the consistency among the triplicate sets. The particle sizes of AuNCs in the TEM image were measured by a software of Nano Measurer 1.2.5 (Fudan University, China). The limit of detection (LOD) was computed by employing the formula 3S/k, where S represents the standard deviation of the blank samples, and k denotes the slope of the calibration curve.

The dmSiO2 with amino groups and the AuNCs with carboxyl groups were separately synthetized by drawing from literature methods with minor adjustments (L. Huang et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2012). The morphologies of AuNCs, dmSiO2 were firstly thoroughly characterized by electron microscopy. TEM study in Fig. 1A reveals that AuNCs are well dispersed with an average size of 2.01 nm (Fig. S1). The HRTEM image (Fig. 1B) revealed that the lattice planes of AuNCs were spaced by 0.235 nm, corresponding to the (111) lattice spacing of the face-centered cubic (fcc) Au (Gao et al., 2022). As displayed in Fig. 1C, dmSiO2 displayed a pomegranate-type architecture with a size of about 240 nm. From the SEM image of dmSiO2 (Fig. 1C), a pore size with tens of nanometers at the outer surface were observed. Besides, the TEM in Fig. 1D showed the precisely defined central–radial pore channels within dmSiO2. The existence of wide radial pore channels is favorable to the loading of nanoparticle guests (Song & Yang, 2015).

The negatively charged AuNCs prepared were readily assembled with positively charged dmSiO2 to boost its fluorescence (Fig. 2A). The SEM image of AuNCs@dmSiO2 in Fig. S2 showed that after the encapsulation of AuNCs into dmSiO2, AuNCs@dmSiO2 kept the similar morphology as dmSiO2 in Fig. 1C expect the thickened Si walls. Fig. 1E, F showed the TEM image of AuNCs@dmSiO2 (Fig. 2F), from which AuNCs (the black dots circled in yellow dash) are uniformly immobilized into the dmSiO2 and a lattice fringe spacing of 0.235 nm attributable to AuNCs was observed (X. Yu et al., 2018). Similarly, from the STEM of AuNCs@dmSiO2 (Fig. 1G), it was found that numerous AuNCs (bright dots) evenly located throughout the dmSiO2 matrix. Additionally, the EDS elemental mapping in Fig. 1H showed the N, S, O, Si, and Au from AuNCs and dmSiO2, indicating that the homogeneous distribution of AuNCs into the dmSiO2. The EDX spectrum in Fig. S3 further indicated that the AuNCs@dmSiO2 were composed of C, N, S, O, Si, and Au and the amounts of Si and Au in AuNCs@dmSiO2 was 19.28 % and 8.42 % (weight percent).

The zeta potential results in Fig. 2
B and C indicated that the dmSiO2 was positively charged (+25.4 mV), and the obtained AuNCs were with strong negative charges (−52.6 mV). After the AuNCs were self-assembled into the pores of dmSiO2 via electrostatic interaction, a neutralized zeta potential of AuNCs@dmSiO2 (−33.9 mV) was observed. Meanwhile, the surface areas of dmSiO2 and AuNCs@dmSiO2 were explored through the measurement of N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. As depicted in Fig. 2D, characteristic type IV isotherms were observed, consistent with previous findings (L. Huang et al., 2018). Besides, after the self-assembly with AuNCs and dmSiO2, a BET surface area of dmSiO2 decreased from 291.8 m2/g to 128.5 m2/g, and the total pore volume decreased from 1.58 cm3/g to 0.99 cm3/g, confirming the loading of AuNCs into the pore of dmSiO2.

The XRD patterns in Fig. 2E showed AuNCs@dmSiO2 nanocomposite exhibited two peaks at 2θ of 22° and 33° that matched with the amorphous silica in dmSiO2 (Velmurugan et al., 2015) and (111) plane of Au fcc structure in AuNCs (Jafari et al., 2017), respectively, further implying the self-assembly of AuNCs and dmSiO2 to form AuNCs@dmSiO2. The characteristic absorption of functional groups of the AuNCs, dmSiO2 and AuNCs@dmSiO2 were shown in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2F). The typical stretching vibrations of CO at 1401 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1645 cm−1, stretching NH vibration at 1536 cm−1, and OH stretching at 3425 cm−1 were observed in the FT-IR spectrum of AuNCs (Y. S. He et al., 2018). As for dmSiO2, the bending and symmetric stretching vibrations of SiO bond (at 462 and 801 cm−1, respectively), and antisymmetric stretching vibration of Si-O-Si (1081 cm−1) can be observed (Holtzer et al., 2011; Kamarudin et al., 2013). The AuNCs@dmSiO2 displayed these specific absorption peaks originated from AuNCs and dmSiO2, verifying the successful self-assembly of AuNCs@dmSiO2 nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characteristics and enhancement mechanism of AuNCs@dmSiO2
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence features and enhancement mechanism of AuNCs@dmSiO2 were comprehensively studied. As revealed in Fig. 3A after assembling AuNCs with non-fluorescent dmSiO2, the FL emission of AuNCs was markedly enhanced about 3-time along with a blue-shift of FL emission peak from 604 to 598 nm, and an obvious fluorescent color change from weak orange-pink to intense orange was observed. The CIE coordinates of AuNCs changed from (0.5342, 0.4557) to (0.5144, 0.4569) after forming the AuNCs@dmSiO2 (Fig. 3B). Besides, an obvious blue shift can also be observed in the FL excitation bands of AuNCs@dmSiO2 compared with pure AuNCs (Fig. S4). Fig. S5 showed that the emission spectrum of AuNCs@dmSiO2 was centered at 598 nm with excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 to 470 nm, proving the excitation wavelength independence feature of AuNCs@dmSiO2. As revealed in Fig. 3C, the self-assembly between AuNCs and dmSiO2 can be quickly completed within 5 min. Fig. 3
D and E showed that with the increase of dmSiO2 to AuNCs ratio (by weight), the FL of AuNCs firstly gradually increased and then decreased when the ratio was over 5:1. Correspondingly, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of AuNCs@dmSiO2 was enhanced to 6.10 %, representing a 2.4-fold increase compared to AuNCs (QY = 2.52 %). Besides, as shown in Fig. 3F and Table S1, the average FL lifetime was calculated to improve from 2.04 μs (AuNCs) to 9.70 μs (AuNCs@dmSiO2). The stability of AuNCs@dmSiO2 was also examined. Besides, Fig. S6A showed that the AuNCs@dmSiO2 maintained stable under pH 4.0–8.0. As revealed in Fig. S6B and S6C, it can be seen that the AuNCs@dmSiO2 preserves around 90 % and 91 % of the initial intensity after the 60 min Xe lamp irradiation, and in the 100 mM high salt environment, respectively. Fig. S6D showed that the FL intensity of AuNCs@dmSiO2 still kept stable after 60 d storage. The above results indicates that the confinement effect of dmSiO2 on AuNCs can effectively enhance the fluorescence properties of AuNCs and endowed the high stability to AuNCs, greatly enhancing the application prospects of AuNCs@dmSiO2 for sensing applications.

Based on previous investigations, when the photo-induced electrons and holes of the fluorescent guest material is confined by the host matrix with a broad band gap, the excited electron and hole are limited to transfer to the conduction band and valence band, respectively, promoting the recombination of electron and hole and resulting in the enhanced fluorescence properties (Li, Zhang, et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2022). Therefore, to verify the confinement effect of dmSiO2, the band edge placements of AuNCs and dmSiO2 were investigated. From Fig. 3
G and H and the detailed calculations processes exhibited in supporting information, for AuNCs, the HOMO and LUMO energies were calculated to be −6.48 and − 4.21 eV (vs. vacuum), respectively. For dmSiO2, the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) were calculated to be −8.16 and − 3.88 eV (vs. vacuum). Consequently, as revealed in Fig. 3I, the excited electron and hole of AuNCs are unable to transfer to the conduction and valence bands of dmSiO2, respectively, and will be trapped in the energy well of dmSiO2, which promotes the recombination of electrons and holes of AuNCs to eventually boost the fluorescence performance of AuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design mechanism of the enhanced sensing platform for OPs
ce:section.ce:para: By integrated the verified confinement effect enhanced FL of AuNCs and the abundant porous property of dmSiO2, a AuNCs@dmSiO2 based fluorescent strategy with the advantages of strong adsorption and enrichment ability for the analytes was developed for enhanced sensing OPs (Scheme 1). In detail, the essential principle of this sensor for the detection of OPs consists of the following three aspects (Fig. 4A). (1) The AuNCs@dmSiO2 could be quenched by Cu2+ with surprising sensitivity. (2) The quenching process could be efficiently inhibited by the enzymatic product (TCh) of AChE due to its higher affinity for complexing with Cu2+. (3) The introduction of OPs irreversibly obstructed the catalytic activity of AChE (Suwannachat et al., 2024), resulting in the inhibition of ATCh hydrolysis, thereby maintaining the Cu2+-triggered fluorescence signal. Therefore, benefiting from the integrated excellent fluorescence and porous properties of AuNCs@dmSiO2 nanocomposite, an enhanced on-site monitoring system for OPs was constructed.

The following experiments were systematically conducted to validate that this sensing system possesses the capability to enhance the sensing sensitivity. As shown in Fig. 4B, when same content of Cu2+ (10 μM) was mixed with AuNCs and AuNCs@dmSiO2, the decrease in FL intensity of AuNCs@dmSiO2 was around 3-time as the FL intensity of AuNCs with a more apparent FL color change. Based on the previous reports, the quenching mechanism for Cu2+-regulated fluorescence quenching of AuNCs should be referred to the coordination of Cu2+ with the carboxyl group from the ligand GSH of AuNCs to form Cu2+@AuNCs complexes, which might block the ligand-Au charge transfer and establish the effective charge transfer from AuNCs to Cu2+ (Babaee et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). Fig. 4C and Table S1 showed the Cu2+ caused the fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs reducing form 9.70 μs to 8.62 μs. Compared with the displayed monodisperse AuNCs in Fig. 1A, Fig. 4D displayed the addition of Cu2+ caused the clear aggregation morphology of AuNCs, which undoubtedly validated the combination of Cu2+ with AuNCs.

Under the demonstrated optimized conditions for Cu2+ sensing in Fig. S7, AuNCs@dmSiO2 and AuNCs were employed for Cu2+ sensing for comparison, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3
E and G, the FL intensity of AuNCs@dmSiO2 and AuNCs both decreased gradually with increasing Cu2+ concentration from 0 to 20.0 μM. The linear range for Cu2+ detection with AuNCs as a probe was 0.1–3.0 μM (Fig. 3H), and the detection limit (LOD) was derived to be 0.035 μM (35 nM). Delightedly, by applying AuNCs@dmSiO2 for Cu2+ sensing, the linear range was 0.005–3.0 μM (Fig. 3F) with an about 10-time lower LOD of 0.0028 μM (2.8 nM).

The results indicated that the participation of dmSiO2 for constructing nanocomposites greatly improved the sensitivity for target Cu2+ detection. The improved sensitivity of AuNCs@dmSiO2 for target Cu2+ detection can be attributed to the porous structure of dmSiO2. In detail, the porous structure of dmSiO2 can provide a larger surface area, thereby increasing the contact area between the sensor and target substances in the environment, making the sensor more sensitive to target substances. Besides, the dmSiO2 matrix with abundant pores can act as a high-performance adsorbent, which can effectively adsorb and enrich the targets to facilitate the diffusion and transportation of target substances, thereby enhancing the response sensitivity for the analytes (Greathouse et al., 2021; Jang et al., 2020).

Accordingly, the surprising sensitivity of Cu2+ can enable the development of an enhanced on-site monitoring system for OPs. First of all, Fig. S8 displayed that ATCh, AChE, or ATCh/AChE showed no impact on the fluorescence of AuNCs@dmSiO2, and Fig. S9 displayed that OPs had no influence on the fluorescence of AuNCs@dmSiO2 or AuNCs@dmSiO2/Cu2+ system, which ensures the specificity response of the system. As revealed in Fig. 4I, the fluorescence recovery of AuNCs@dmSiO2/Cu2+ was triggered in the presence of ATCh/AChE, while ATCh or AChE could not recover the fluorescence due to a combination of Cu2+ and the generated TCh. As expected, in comparison to AChE+ATCh system, the presence of OPs could suppress the fluorescence recovery through inhibiting the activity of AChE to hinder the formation of TCh. From the above results, it was feasible to construct a plainly sensing platform for highly sensitive OPs.

The sulfhydryl group in the the AChE product (TCh) can effectively coordinate with Cu2+ to prevent the quenching of AuNCs@dmSiO2. OPs can hinder the formation of TCh based on the efficient inhibitory effect of OPs on AChE, which preserves the Cu2+-triggered FL quenching of the system ( Fig. 5
A). As proof-of-concept, this sensing method was employed for OPs detection with paraoxon as a model representative (Chang et al., 2024) to verify the recognition ability of this sensor. In this designed method, AChE was employed as an antenna for specifically recognizing OPs. Accordingly, this system was first investigated for the analysis capability toward AChE. As shown in Fig. 5
B, under the optimized conditions for enzymatic reaction (shown in Fig. S10A–D), FL intensity of AuNCs@dmSiO2 at 598 nm gradually increased in correlation with the AChE concentration ranging from 0 to 18.0 U/L. Fig. 5
C showed that the linear equation between the FL intensity ratio (F/F0, F and F0 represented the FL intensity of AuNCs@dmSiO2/Cu2+/ATCh with and without AChE) and AChE concentration (0.2–12.0 U/L) was F/F0 = 0.900 + 0.195[AChE], (U/L, R2 = 0.989) with a LOD of 0.048 U/L, manifesting the sensitive response of this method to AChE.

Taking advantage of good performance toward AChE, this AuNCs@dmSiO2/Cu2+/AChE/ATCh sensing system was utilized for monitoring OPs by selecting paraoxon as a model ( Fig. 5
D). As displayed in Fig. 5
E, as the concentration of OPs (paraoxon) increased from 0 to 100.0 ng/mL, the FL intensity was gradually decreased, which can be attributed to the Cu2+-triggered quenching of AuNCs@dmSiO2. There was a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.991) between the intensity ratio (F0/F, F and F0 represented the FL intensity of AuNCs@dmSiO2/Cu2+/AChE/ATCh with and without paraoxon) and concentration of paraoxon was obtained within the range of 0.05 to 25.0 ng/mL in Fig. 5
F with a calculated LOD of 0.032 ng/mL. The linear regression equation was F0/F = 1.061 + 0.046[paraoxon], ng/mL. Additionally, this assay was comparable or more sensitive compared to other reported OPs assays (Table S2), making it suitable for further applications in actual application circumstances.

For evaluating the specificity of this method, a series of potential interfering substances, including other common types of pesticides (neonicotinoid pesticide, aniline pesticide, and pyrethroid pesticide), ions, amino acids, and other molecules, were further studied (Jin et al., 2019). Fig. 5
G showed that compared with the remarkable response by paraoxon, these control interference substances caused inconspicuous variation on the system. Results in Fig. 5
H revealed that almost the same response to paraoxon was obtained when these tested substances coexisted in the sensing system. The good selectivity and anti-interference ability of this strategy for target OPs sensing are undoubtedly validated, which benefits from the high specificity of AChE toward OPs and AuNCs@dmSiO2/Cu2+-assisted target analyte identification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Constructing a portable kit for on-site monitoring of OPs
ce:section.ce:para: This proposed assay as well as other methods in Table S2 could satisfy the requirements of highly precise analysis detect OPs, but still required commercial analytical instruments, which will restrict the portable point-of-care testing of the OPs (B. R. Jin et al., 2024). Thus, it would make sense to fabricate kit with portability for on-site monitoring OPs analysis. For this purpose, this fluorescent method was applied in constructing a portable test kit by integrating with a smartphone-based homemade 3D-printing portable device (Fig. 6A). As revealed in Fig. 6A, the test kit is composed of a AuNCs@dmSiO2-embedded test swab and a sample reaction tube. The test swab soaked in the solution tube consisting of AuNCs@dmSiO2/Cu2+/AChE/ATCh reaction system with different concentration of OP (paraoxon) and then was taken out for photographing. As expected, as depicted in Fig. 6B, a series of fluorescent color changes of darkening were observed on the test swab with OPs increasing, indicating the test swab can achieve distinguishing OPs by the naked eye. Subsequently, the photo color of was converted to RGB values with a smartphone APP (Color Collection) for the quantification of OPs. Fig. 6C showed that a good linear relationship between the R/(G + B) value and OP paraoxon concentration was obtained in the range of 2.0–70.0 ng/mL (linear regression eq. R/(G + B) = 0.656–0.00401[paraoxon], ng/mL, R2 = 0.987) with a LOD of 0.65 ng/mL. Accordingly, a smartphone-assisted portable kit was constructed and verified to supply a visual, equipment-free, portable tool for on-site monitoring of OPs, which provided effective technical safeguards to avoid food poisoning and long-term health risks caused by OPs residues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: OPs analysis in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The OPs monitoring is of significance to ensure food quality and human health. In this case, real samples that are usually binding with OPs, including tap-water, apple, and cabbage were employed as research targets to verify the practicability of this method (Li et al., 2023; Reddicherla Umapathi et al., 2021). Above all, Fig. S11A and S11B showed that the apple juice, cabbage samples, or tap water all displayed no influence on the AuNCs@dmSiO2 and AuNCs@dmSiO2/Cu2+ systems, eliminating the interference from flour samples. In addition, a UV–vis analysis method, with a good relationship (R2 = 0.970), was used as a comparison method to confirm the analysis accuracy (Fig. S12). As shown in Table S3, the results obtained from the fluorescent, smartphone-assisted test kit and UV–vis method kept well consistent. Besides, satisfactory recoveries of 94.7 % to 106.7 % were obtained with a relative standard deviation below 5.6 %.

In conclusion, the AuNCs@dmSiO2 with enhanced fluorescence property was designed by simply confining AuNCs into dmSiO2 to strategically construct a fluorometric method for the determination of OPs by selecting paraoxon as a target model. This work exhibited the following distinctive features. (1) A simple and rapid non-covalent electrostatic assembly approach was proposed to synthesize AuNCs@dmSiO2, which exhibited enhanced fluorescence intensity, much higher QY, and prolonged fluorescence lifetime. (2) By integrating the features of AuNCs and dmSiO2, the sensitive identification of Cu2+ by AuNCs, and the high specificity of AChE toward OPs, a sensitive fluorescent analysis platform for OPs (paraoxon) detection was creatively proposed with a LOD of 0.032 ng/mL. (3) A portable test swab was developed for monitoring OPs, which provided a visual and equipment-free tool for on-site detection of OPs. It is believed that the work not only gives enlightening inspirations for constructing high performance AuNCs-based composite material, but also offers an effective avenue for OPs related food safety supervision.
